title: Egocentric Video Data Collection for Robotics and World Modeling
teaser: 1,000+ hours of geographically diverse, research-grade egocentric video delivered under aggressive timelines — platform launched in days, full delivery in weeks.
summary: Partnered with a leading generative AI research lab focused on world modeling and general-purpose robotics. Delivered 1,000+ hours of geographically diverse, research-grade egocentric video under an aggressive timeline. Scaled to approximately 500 global contributors capturing first-person footage of everyday manipulation tasks, walking, driving, and cooking using smartphones, GoPros, and wearable cameras. Requirements evolved week by week, yet the platform launched in days with full delivery in weeks.
challenge: Robotic manipulation and world-model research requires massive volumes of first-person video showing natural human behavior in diverse real-world environments, and no public dataset meets frontier labs' quality or diversity bar. The lab had attempted internal collection but stalled at modest scale due to hardware logistics, contributor recruitment across geographies, and the overhead of enforcing consistent capture quality — resolution, frame rate, scene coverage, and activity diversity — across hundreds of participants. They needed a partner who could launch a production-ready pipeline in days, not months, and adapt to evolving research specifications on a weekly cadence without sacrificing annotation-ready quality standards.
approach:
We launched a production-ready collection workflow within days of engagement. The first step was translating the lab's nuanced research-level movement specifications — specific hand-object interaction patterns, locomotion types, environmental diversity requirements — into contributor-friendly task instructions that non-technical participants could execute reliably. We supported capture across smartphones, GoPros, and wearable cameras, normalizing output formats to a consistent resolution and frame-rate spec.

An in-house QA team ran continuous validation on every submission, checking framing, motion quality, duration compliance, and scene-type metadata. Real-time dashboards enabled dynamic rebalancing across geographies and activity types as the lab's priorities shifted week to week. Coverage spanned household tasks, fine-grained manipulation actions (pouring, cutting, assembling), walking, driving, and cooking — each tagged with structured activity labels.

The operational model was designed for velocity: contributor onboarding took under 48 hours, QA turnaround was same-day, and weekly delivery batches kept the lab's training pipeline fed continuously rather than waiting for a monolithic handoff.

results.label: Time from engagement to platform launch
Total time to full dataset delivery

impact: The dataset became primary training data for the lab's world-modeling and robotic manipulation research. Geographic and demographic diversity across approximately 500 contributors reduced the distribution shift between training data and real-world deployment environments — a known failure mode when models are trained on data from a single region or demographic. Weekly delivery cadence allowed the lab to begin training runs during collection rather than after, compressing their research iteration cycle by weeks.
whyItMatters: For robotics and world models, progress depends on how quickly real-world data can be collected and put to use. Foundation models for embodied AI need millions of diverse egocentric frames showing natural human behavior across varied environments. Collecting this data requires coordinating hundreds of people across geographies, managing heterogeneous capture hardware, and enforcing research-grade quality at scale — problems that are operational, not algorithmic. This engagement demonstrated that large-scale first-person video collection can be both fast and cost-effective without sacrificing the quality that frontier labs require.

faqs.question: What camera hardware was used for egocentric capture?
faqs.answer: Contributors used smartphones, GoPros, and wearable cameras depending on the task and environment. All output was normalized to a consistent resolution and frame-rate specification regardless of capture device. We validated each device's output against quality baselines before a contributor began recording.

faqs.question: How did you ensure consistent quality across hundreds of global contributors?
faqs.answer: Every submission passed through an automated and human QA pipeline within 24 hours. Automated checks validated framing, motion quality, duration compliance, and metadata completeness. An in-house QA team reviewed flagged submissions and provided specific remediation instructions. Real-time dashboards tracked pass rates by geography and activity type, enabling rapid intervention when quality dipped in any segment.

faqs.question: What types of activities were captured?
faqs.answer: Coverage spanned household tasks, fine-grained manipulation actions (pouring, cutting, assembling), walking, driving, and cooking. Activities were selected collaboratively with the research team and evolved weekly as their priorities shifted. Each clip was tagged with structured activity labels and environment metadata.

faqs.question: How did you handle evolving research requirements?
faqs.answer: The platform was designed for weekly specification changes from day one. Task instructions, activity taxonomies, and quality thresholds were parameterized so they could be updated without rebuilding the pipeline. When the lab shifted priorities — for example, requesting more driving footage or adding a new manipulation subcategory — updated instructions were pushed to contributors within 48 hours.

faqs.question: How quickly can this be replicated for a new engagement?
faqs.answer: Platform launch takes days, not weeks. The core infrastructure — contributor onboarding, capture apps, QA pipelines, delivery formatting — is reusable. The primary variable is task-specific calibration: translating a new lab's research specifications into contributor instructions and QA criteria, which typically requires a 1-2 week pilot phase.
